data.title: Contractor was warned over risks a week before deadly fire hit Hong Kong estate
data.description:
Hong Kong labour authorities had warned the contractor working on Wang Fuk Court’s renovation about a fire hazard just a week before the housing estate in Tai Po was engulfed in the city’s worst blaze in decades, the Post has learned.
In the aftermath of the devastating blaze that has so far claimed 65 lives, some residents said the fire alarms did not go off while an engineer warned that the flats destroyed in the inferno might not be habitable again.
In a reply to the Post’s queries, the Labour Department said on Thursday it had inspected Wang Fuk Court 16 time since July last year, and as recently as November 20.
The inspections included verifying whether the scaffolding nets met safety standards.
Less than a week ago, it reminded the contractor of the need to implement appropriate fire safety measures.
The department said there were unsafe conditions for working at height, for which three warnings were issued.
Ho Wing-ip, a veteran electrical and building services engineer, noted that many residents said they did not hear the fire alarms and questioned why the safety system was not functioning during a renovation project that posed clear fire hazards.
“If the system was not working, did they [the contractor] give the fire services a heads-up that renovation [work] on the system was under way, so that the department could make special arrangements in case of an emergency?” Ho asked.
The blaze first broke out at Wang Cheong House at 2.51pm on Wednesday and rapidly tore through the scaffolding surrounding the buildings, prompting the Fire Services Department to upgrade it successively to a No 3 alarm blaze at 3.02pm and No 4 at 3.34pm before escalating it to the maximum threat level No 5 at 6.22pm.
In addition to the dry conditions and strong winds, the flammable scaffolding nets and polystyrene foam boards covering the windows likely fuelled the flames.
Retiree Chan Kwong-tak, an 83-year-old resident of the estate, told the Post that the fire alarms did not go off, adding that there were no evacuation arrangements or instructions for residents in the building.
“If there were elderly fast asleep, they would have burned to death. We tried to alert those around us,” Chan said. “Of course, some of the workers [on site] smoked, but they should be careful, especially when welding.”
A former head of security at the housing estate, identified only by his surname Wong, said that he and other residents had seen workers smoking in public areas of the estate and even while working on the scaffolding.
“The worst thing is that the fire safety and alarm system was always turned off for the convenience of the workers going in and out of the building via the fire exits. The doors won’t be locked and the alarms won’t sound,” Wong said.

Engineer Ho also expressed concerns about the structural integrity of the buildings, particularly the rebar – steel rods with ridges used to reinforce concrete – which would be weakened when exposed to heat of about 550 to 600 degrees Celsius.
My intuition is that there is only a slim chance the buildings that burned throughout the night can be lived in again,” he said. “It’s highly likely that all the plasterwork, fittings, mechanical systems, and lifts are no longer functional, with only the bare shell remaining.”
According to Ho, it might ultimately cost less to rebuild than renovate, but such a decision went beyond engineering considerations, and would include questions such as where the residents would be housed and how insurance would be handled.
The 41-year-old Wang Fuk Court comprises eight blocks with more than 1,900 flats housing an estimated 4,000 residents.
The fire, which ripped through seven of the eight blocks, was brought under control as of 6pm on Thursday.
All the blocks were covered with bamboo scaffolding and mesh under an ongoing HK$330 million (US$44.4 million) renovation.

data.title: Deadly Hong Kong fire leaves residents distraught as they mourn loss, search for missing
data.description:
An elderly Hongkonger desperately begged for help to find his six-month-old granddaughter who remained missing more than a day after Wednesday’s deadly fire engulfed a Tai Po estate.
The man broke down as he pleaded for help outside a shelter on Thursday to locate his granddaughter, who lived on the 21st floor of Block F of Wang Cheong House, while the baby’s grandmother was also missing.
“I’ve checked all the hospitals – no sign of them,” he said. “Police told me to register their details and I did. I just wish there was more I could do. Maybe they’re safe somewhere.”
“It breaks my heart to imagine the baby hasn’t had food for 20 hours,” he added.
The deadly blaze that swept through Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po left residents distraught and heartbroken as they mourned the loss of their loved ones and searched for those still missing, while the full-scale rescue operation continued.


The fire, which has claimed at least 65 lives, has become a haunting memory for many, with the smell of burnt plastic still lingering in the air.
The girlfriend of firefighter Ho Wai-ho, who was killed in the fire, posted a heartfelt message on social media on Thursday to mourn his death.
“I really really want to be able to hold your hand again,” she wrote, adding that she “could not accept” what had happened.
“Thank you for everyone’s well wishes, I have read them all,” she said. “My superhero has finished his mission and returned to Krypton. You’re my pride!”
A retiree in her 60s who gave her surname as Ho recalled her family’s narrow escape a day earlier.
“We were on our way to the restaurant when we noticed the nearby building was on fire,” she said.
“We brought our mother down in her wheelchair as quickly as we could because we knew the smoke might affect the operation of the lift.”
The family spent the night at a relative’s home, she added, noting they were uncertain about the future.
“The hope of going back soon is getting smaller and smaller. We can still see how badly it’s burning,” she said. “We haven’t even thought about a long-term plan yet.”
Distressing scenes showing frightened residents in desperate need of support were captured and circulated.

One included a helper caring for a three-month-old baby who called a friend and cried for help, according to an audio clip released by a social media influencer known in the city’s Filipino community.
“Our building is on fire … we’re stuck inside the room … I’m holding the baby … please help us,” the helper said while choking. She was later rescued.
Kwong Fuk Community Hall in Tai Po opened on Thursday to families with missing people from the fire to identify their loved ones based on photos of bodies.
Some residents were seen leaving the hall in tears.
“The staff warned me that the photos are horrifying, so I don’t dare look at them. I’m contacting other relatives to come and help,” said a woman surnamed Wong who sought to identify her aunt living in Wang Cheong House.
Meanwhile, more than 24 hours after the fire was first reported at 2.51pm on Wednesday and soon grew into an inferno, full-scale rescue efforts were still under way on Thursday.
Smoke continued to billow from all levels of the affected buildings at dawn after a night’s burning. Fires at four of the seven residential buildings had been extinguished in the afternoon while the flames at the remaining three were under control.
In all, the Fire Services Department deployed more than 300 fire engines and rescue vehicles, mobilising 1,250 firefighters.
Director of Fire Services Andy Yeung Yan-kin earlier said officers had discovered styrofoam material inside the burning buildings, which caused the fire to spread more rapidly within the blocks and ignite flats through the corridors.
Firefighters were only able to enter the buildings from the ground floor during the rescue, with a major difficulty being the number of floors that were on fire, he said.

The wife of a firefighter uploaded an emotional WhatsApp exchange between herself and her husband to a mainland Chinese social media platform.
Her husband told her that “a lot of people cannot be saved”, and described the blaze as “a huge fire, so hot and burning so fiercely”.
When the wife urged him to be careful, he replied: “I have no choice but to go in.”
In a statement on Wednesday evening, President Xi Jinping expressed his condolences and offered his sympathies to the victims’ families and all affected residents, as he “called for all-out efforts to extinguish the fire and minimise casualties and losses”.
Condolences from mainland Chinese residents poured in on social media as they conveyed sympathies as well as shock.
“The footage of the Tai Po fire is indeed shocking, and I can’t believe this can happen in Hong Kong!” a user in Beijing posted on Thursday.
Shanghai student Wei Huazhen, 20, who was visiting Hong Kong for a week, brought flowers to the site of the fire on Thursday to pay his respects.
“When I was little, there was a big fire in my hometown, Shanghai,” Wei said. “This incident reminded me of that, and I wanted to take the opportunity to pay tribute to the deceased.”
As the tragedy gripped the hearts of city residents as well as those across the border, local and mainland celebrities also posted messages of support on social media, and major entertainment events were cancelled or adjusted.
The organiser of the 2025 MAMA Awards, to be held on Friday and Saturday at Kai Tak Stadium, on Thursday said the event’s performances were being carefully prepared with “restrained production” while extending condolences to those affected by the fire.

data.title: Moore Threads’ IPO frenzy fires up China’s home-grown GPU drive
data.description:
Chinese onshore investor frenzy for artificial intelligence chips has intensified, with the initial public offering (IPO) of Beijing-based graphics processing unit (GPU) maker Moore Threads drawing more than 4,000 times subscription from retail investors this week.
The red-hot demand for Moore Threads highlights China’s determination to build home-grown alternatives to Nvidia’s chips, and is expected to accelerate listing plans for other domestic GPU developers, from MetaX to Biren Technology.
Cambricon Technologies, a Shanghai-listed AI chipmaker, has also become one of the most sought-after stocks in the city, with its share price doubling in 2025.
Backed by tech giants including Tencent Holdings and ByteDance, Moore Threads opened lottery-style subscriptions on Monday at an IPO price of 114.28 yuan.
Online orders from retail investors exceeded the number of shares on offer by more than 4,000 times, resulting in a final allotment rate of just 0.036 per cent, according to a company filing on Wednesday.
On Shanghai’s Nasdaq-style Star Market, qualified institutional investors receive proportionate allocations, while retail investors subscribe through a system akin to a lucky draw.
Moore Threads raised about 8 billion yuan, valuing the company at 53.7 billion yuan. Its debut date has yet to be finalised.
Chinese authorities have been guiding private capital towards supporting the country’s drive for technological self-sufficiency, particularly in semiconductors. It took Moore Threads just 88 days from filing in June to receiving approval, making it one of the fastest IPOs to secure the go ahead.

Moore Threads was founded in 2020 by James Zhang Jianzhong, a former Nvidia vice-president and head of its China operations, who spent 14 years at the US chip giant before striking out on his own.
Many of the company’s early hires also came from Nvidia and Advanced Micro Devices, giving the start-up instant credibility and helping it attract investment from prominent backers such as Shenzhen Capital Group, Sequoia China, GGV Capital, ByteDance and Tencent.
In total, it counted nearly 90 shareholders ahead of the IPO, according to its prospectus.
Despite being added to the US government’s Entity List – the trade blacklist that restricts exports of US technology to specific foreign companies deemed a US national security risk – Moore Threads has rolled out four generations of GPU architecture: Sudi, Chunxiao, Quyuan and Pinghu.
The company reported revenue of 438.5 million yuan in 2024, representing a compound annual growth rate of more than 200 per cent over the past three years, alongside a net loss of 1.6 billion yuan.
It has yet to turn a profit because of heavy research and development spending and inventory build-ups. In the first half of 2025, revenue rose to 701.8 million yuan, while net losses narrowed to 270.1 million yuan.

data.title: China, EU push Nexperia and Chinese owner to end ‘corporate dispute’ amid chip concerns
data.description:
Both China and the European Union have signalled that a dispute over semiconductor maker Nexperia should be resolved at the corporate level, according to China’s Ministry of Commerce, as affected countries continue to seek a solution to supply-chain disruptions.
The push for a corporate resolution came after two separate video conferences that Chinese Commerce Minister Wang Wentao had on Wednesday – with German Economy Minister Katherina Reiche and European Commissioner for Trade Maros Sefcovic.
“Both sides agree that … Nexperia Netherlands and Nexperia China should promptly engage in constructive communication regarding their internal corporate disputes and find an effective, long-term solution,” Chinese commerce authorities said on Thursday, in a statement following Wang’s digital face time with Reiche.
Acknowledging that the global semiconductor supply chain remains highly fragile, Wang reiterated China’s position that even though Beijing welcomed The Hague’s decision to suspend the executive order that allowed the Dutch government to take control of Nexperia, the Dutch side still needs to revoke the order and withdraw the court ruling – which Beijing sees as the root causes of this issue, according to the statement.
Representatives for the German government did not immediately reply to the Post’s request for comment.
In the call between Wang and Sefcovic, they pledged to “jointly urge” the entities to start a dialogue and work towards restoring stability to the global semiconductor supply chain, an earlier Chinese statement said on Wednesday.


Wang pressed Brussels to “urge the Dutch government to put forward a constructive solution”, according to the ministry.
Sefcovic acknowledged Beijing’s efforts to prevent a production crisis and expressed a willingness to continue easing tensions, the Chinese side said.
Olof Gill, an EU trade spokesman, told the Post on Wednesday that the talk was “constructive and productive”, noting that technical discussions would continue in an effort to “fully re-establish” supply.
Frans-Paul van der Putten, founder of Dutch consulting firm ChinaGeopolitics, said that China’s push for companies to take the lead may be an attempt to downgrade the diplomatic urgency while ramping up pressure on The Hague to restore Nexperia’s unity.
The diplomatic flurry aims to mitigate supply-chain risks for entities dependent on Nexperia, especially German carmakers. Chinese owner Wingtech Technology and Dutch subsidiary Nexperia Netherlands have traded barbs in recent weeks, accusing each other of endangering the supply chain.
Wingtech said at the weekend that it had received no meaningful reply after reaching out to Nexperia’s headquarters for talks.
Nexperia did not immediately respond to the Post’s request for comment.
Before speaking with Wang, Sefcovic met with Dutch Economy Minister Vincent Karremans, along with US Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick and US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer on Monday.
All sides agreed to work closely on the Nexperia issue and to continue working towards a stable global chip supply chain, “with attention to mutual strategic dependencies”, according to the Dutch minister.
While Wingtech has vowed to use legal means to regain control, legal experts have warned that a court battle could drag on for more than three years.
Additional reporting by Finbarr Bermingham

data.title: Why advances in conventional weapons tech should worry China, Russia and US
data.description:
In nuclear war, the long-held assumption of mutual assured destruction no longer exists. Welcome to the age of permanent strategic instability, where superpowers mix fast and accurate nuclear and non-nuclear missiles in the same arsenal. The risk of war is greater than many realise.
The scale of catastrophe is also significant. A potential nuclear winter is estimated to amount to billions of deaths from starvation after years of global crop failures. Such risks and consequences are disastrously underestimated.
Nuclear powers have long sought the ability to fight and win a nuclear war. For the United States and its allies, that temptation exists even without the use of nuclear weapons. In 2024, we argued how the US could use non-nuclear strikes to negate Chinese and Russian nuclear deterrents.
Ryan Snyder at Princeton University similarly concludes that the US’ “long-range air- and sea-based precision conventional cruise missiles possess lethality against missile silos comparable to [US] nuclear ballistic missiles”, well above 90 per cent. Eli Sanchez at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology finds that near-future US hypersonic weapons can defeat silo-based intercontinental ballistic missiles (ICBMs) with “comparable efficacy” to nuclear warheads.
Long-range precision weapons blur the line between nuclear and conventional warfare. They can destroy the same targets as nuclear weapons without the stigma of nuclear use. This turns conventional missiles into credible substitutes for silo-busting nuclear strikes and undermines the retaliatory nuclear credibility of peer rivals.
A glimpse of this logic came when US President Donald Trump recently deployed two US nuclear submarines to “appropriate regions” in response to Russian threats. Trump publicly boasted of their undetectable presence.

For Chinese and Russian planners, the implication is clear: in a crisis, US ballistic-missile submarines could be positioned against them. Prudent Chinese and Russian analysts must assume the possibility of facing US submarines equipped with non-nuclear warheads on their ballistic Trident II or D5 missiles.
More than a decade ago, Washington began developing its conventional Trident modification programme. The US military’s conventional prompt global strike programme has long considered and recommended the modification of Trident missiles for non-nuclear use.
Although Congress rejected funding for such efforts in the 2000s, the programme could easily be operational in the near future, given US capabilities. Moreover, US lawmakers have encouraged the Pentagon to shift towards broader terms for the conventional prompt global strike programme, which allows related efforts to continue with funding.
Also, there hasn’t been the kind of congressional or public pressure that normally accompanies an outright termination of a major weapons programme, which suggests a quiet preservation of the option.


Even if not operational today, the programme’s technical feasibility and mere presence in American planning documents shape the perceptions and choices of adversaries.
Launched from submarines, a conventionally armed Trident missile would be ideal for first-strike counterforce missions. Two such submarines could theoretically field some non-nuclear warheads within minutes to disable China’s core strategic missiles and command centres.
China and Russia must reckon with the American and allied arsenal of long-range conventional strike systems that threaten deeply buried and hardened sites like China’s “underground Great Wall”, potentially neutralising ICBMs before they can even launch. Chinese analysts are likely to operate under worst-case assumptions: that the US either retains this capability or could rapidly deploy it.
Beijing is likewise accelerating its nuclear build-up to narrow the gap between its estimated 600 warheads and the US’ deployed arsenal, which is more than double China’s. While it has been long committed to “minimum deterrence” and a strict no-first-use doctrine, China could very well shift towards a launch-under-attack orientation.

Epitomising this phase of emerging tripartite tension is the Golden Dome initiative: a proposed US space- and ground-based missile defence shield aimed at countering potential strikes from China and Russia. For Beijing, this is not a defensive project but a direct challenge to its second-strike credibility.
It is likely to speed up China’s nuclear modernisation efforts, such as the development of its DF-26 and DF-41 vehicles or experimentation with early launch doctrines aimed at ensuring retaliatory survival. In seeking invulnerability, the US may ignite a new arms race.
These dynamics are not sudden. In 2014, President Xi Jinping warned that emerging US rapid-strike weapons could “fundamentally transform the traditional array of offence and defence in warfare”.
These systems erode China’s assured retaliation posture and risk pushing Beijing towards more ambiguous, even pre-emptive, deterrence behaviour. In 2013, Russian President Vladimir Putin similarly warned that long-range missile development was destabilising the strategic balance.


Sceptics may dismiss such claims, yet the underlying logic is difficult to ignore: if one state can hold another’s nuclear forces at risk conventionally, the foundation of mutual vulnerability – the core assumption of deterrence – crumbles.
This is where the New START treaty’s limits are laid bare. Washington argues that long-range conventional missiles lie outside the treaty’s scope, which expires in February 2026. However, Moscow wants any future arms control framework to include conventional weapons. This friction underscores the emerging strategic disorder at a time when China appears convinced that a new era of parity has arrived.
Washington’s plans to resume nuclear testing after three decades further underscore the erosion of restraint, even without considering conventional capabilities.
The first step towards enduring stability is acknowledgement. Washington, Beijing and Moscow should recognise that rapid expansion of precision conventional strike capabilities does not deter war, but rather invites miscalculation. Rapid advances in conventional strike technologies could open a new Pandora’s box.

data.title: Trump’s Rolex gift: Swiss probe bribery claims after tariff deal
data.description:
Two Swiss lawmakers have asked prosecutors to examine whether gifts reportedly given to US President Donald Trump – including a Rolex watch and a gold bar - by Swiss business leaders may have violated Switzerland’s anti-bribery laws.
Switzerland this month struck a framework agreement with the US to cut a 39 per cent tariff on Swiss goods to 15 per cent. The deal came 10 days after a delegation of Swiss business leaders visited Trump in the Oval Office, bearing the gifts, according to Swiss newspaper Blick, US news site Axios, and other media reports.
Raphael Mahaim and Greta Gysin, both Green Party members of parliament, said in a letter to the public prosecutor that the legality of the gifts should be assessed by the judicial system.
“The end does not justify all means, especially when respect for important provisions of our legal order is at stake,” they wrote, asking the prosecutor to determine whether the gifts violated the Swiss Criminal Code. The public prosecutor did not immediately respond to a request for comment on Thursday.
Executives of MSC, Rolex, Partners Group, Mercuria, Richemont, and MKS took part in the meeting with Trump, according to a statement seen by Reuters.

Rolex declined to comment. The White House did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
When asked for comment, Alfred Gantner, a co-founder of Partners Group who attended the meeting, said public-private collaboration had delivered a much-needed resolution to the Swiss-US tariff dispute.
The other companies did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
Under Swiss law, any person can report an offence to a criminal justice authority, which then decides whether criminal proceedings are initiated.
Under Swiss law, offering a foreign public official an “undue advantage” to influence a decision can carry up to five years in prison or a fine.
The lawmakers’ letter listed the gifts but did not name which companies or individuals presented them.

data.title: Kelly Zong steps down as Wahaha chief amid bitter inheritance feud
data.description:
Kelly Zong Fuli, daughter of the late Chinese soft drinks billionaire Zong Qinghou, has stepped down as legal representative, chairwoman and general manager of Wahaha Group, a year after taking over.
Zong was succeeded by Xu Simin, formerly the head of the legal department at an associated company, according to the latest corporate filings on the mainland. She retains her 29 per cent stake in the group.
The 43-year-old took over the beverage empire – known as China’s Coca-Cola – after her father died last year.
Since then, she has been embroiled in a series of disputes regarding trusts her father had established for three extramarital children and a restructuring that excluded the children and their mother, a former senior executive of the group.
She also reportedly cut the salaries of many long-term executives and employees.
The three half-siblings – Jacky Zong, Jessie Zong and Jerry Zong – sued her in Hangzhou and Hong Kong. According to documents filed with the Hong Kong High Court, their late father had entrusted Kelly Zong with setting up three offshore trusts, each worth US$700 million, for them.
They allege she never set up the trusts and withdrew more than US$6 million from the bank account intended as the main funding source. They are seeking to compel the creation of the trusts.

In August, the Hong Kong court ruled the bank account frozen – a decision Kelly Zong disputed but ultimately failed to overturn. The Zhejiang High People’s Court accepted the case in July.
The inheritance battle has shattered the carefully curated image of the Zong family as a model of Chinese traditional values and drawn intense public scrutiny.
Zong Qinghou founded Wahaha in 1987 and over nearly four decades turned it from a school shop into one of the largest beverage groups in the country. He was widely believed to be devoted to his family, even after becoming the mainland’s richest person. Kelly Zong had long been seen as his only child and heiress until the “secret” children were revealed through lawsuits.
The case has also raised alarm among wealthy families across China, prompting many to seek legal advice on estate planning and succession, according to lawyers.
Chinese media reported that Kelly Zong resigned from her positions, and the board had since approved her departure.
Xu joined Hongsheng Group – a drink and packaging-service company Kelly Zong has been in charge of since 2007 – in 2015 and later became head of its legal department.

data.title: Alibaba launches US$537 Quark AI glasses to challenge wearables from Meta, Xiaomi, Rokid
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding on Thursday launched its first artificial intelligence glasses in China, as the tech giant seeks to carve out a solid niche in the growing smart wearables market.
The Quark AI Glasses, named after the company’s Quark AI assistant, include three versions each of the series’ flagship S1 model and the lightweight G1 model. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
“AI glasses are the intelligent devices that truly usher in a revolution in human-computer interaction in the AI era,” Alibaba vice-president Wu Jia said at the product launch. “In this respect, their importance is no less than that of mobile phones.”
With prices starting at 3,799 yuan (US$537), the S1 has a dual-chip system that features Qualcomm’s Snapdragon AR1 processor and a low-power coprocessor. This model provides “intelligent, hands-free assistance for everyday life” – from answering user questions and real-time translation to payments and navigation, Alibaba said at the launch.
The G1 glasses, which cost from 1,899 yuan and weigh 40 grams, share the S1 model’s core hardware, except for the display.
Alibaba’s Quark AI Glasses series is expected to present a fresh challenge to Meta Platforms’ consumer-ready smart glasses with a built-in display – the Meta Ray-Ban Display, released in September – as well as similar smart wearable devices from Chinese firms Rokid and Xiaomi.
The Meta Ray-Ban Display glasses, priced from US$799, feature a full-colour display in the right lens, while the Quark AI Glasses are equipped with a monochrome green display.

The launch came months after Alibaba previewed the Quark glasses in July, which stirred excitement for the company’s first smart wearables.
The company started presales of the glasses in late October on major domestic e-commerce platforms – including Taobao, JD.com and ByteDance-owned Douyin – but had not announced plans for its overseas release.
In the first half of 2025, the global smart glasses market saw shipments more than double, driven mostly by strong demand for Meta’s glasses, along with new offerings from Xiaomi, according to market research firm Counterpoint.
Xiaomi in June launched its own smart glass model that cost 1,999 yuan, while Baidu’s Xiaodu AI Glass Pro went on sale earlier this month priced at 2,299 yuan.
Hangzhou-based AI and augmented reality start-up Rokid earlier this month introduced lightweight AI glasses priced from 2,199 yuan. The product was developed in partnership with Chinese eyewear brand Bolon.
Compared with its domestic rivals, Alibaba on Thursday touted the deep integration of its Quark AI Glasses with its extensive app ecosystem – from mapping service Amap and online shopping marketplace Taobao to online travel agency Fliggy and mobile payment platform Alipay, operated by fintech affiliate Ant Group.
Alibaba said it was also working with external partners to “deliver a unified experience across shopping, travel, payments, music and productivity”. The partnerships include Tencent Holdings’ QQ Music and NetEase Cloud Music.

data.title: Macron says France to restore voluntary military service amid Russia’s looming threat
data.description:
French President Emmanuel Macron unveiled a new national military service plan on Thursday as France seeks to bolster its armed forces to address growing concerns over Russia’s threat to European nations beyond the war in Ukraine.
Macron announced that volunteers aged 18 and 19 will start serving next year in a 10-month new military service programme.
“A new national service is set to be gradually established, starting from next summer,” Macron said in a speech at the Varces military base, in the French Alps.
Young volunteers will serve in France’s mainland and overseas territories only, not in France’s military operations abroad, Macron said.
Earlier this year, Macron announced his intention to provide French youth with a new option to voluntarily serve in the military. Conscription, which France ended in 1996, is not being considered. He explained then that France seeks to boost its defences as Russia’s war in Ukraine puts the European continent at “great risk”.
“The day that you send a signal of weakness to Russia – which for 10 years has made a strategic choice to become an imperial power again, that’s to say advance wherever we are weak – well, it will continue to advance,” he told radio RTL on Tuesday.

Macron has announced €6.5 billion (US$7.6 billion) in extra military spending in the next two years.
He said France will aim to spend €64 billion in annual defence spending in 2027, the last year of his second term. That would be double the €32 billion in annual spending when he became president in 2017.
France’s military currently comprises around 200,000 active personnel and over 40,000 reservists, making it the second largest in the European Union, just behind Poland. France wants to increase the number of reservists to 100,000 by 2030.
France’s new army chief of staff, General Fabien Mandon, last week sent a warning about the nation’s need to get prepared to “lose its children” in the event of a potential conflict with Russia – words that prompted an outcry across the political spectrum.
Russia annexed 20 per cent of Georgia’s territory in 2008, Ukraine’s Crimean Peninsula in 2014 and launched a full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022, General Mandon said.

“Unfortunately, Russia today, based on the information I have access to, is preparing for a confrontation with our countries by 2030. It is organising itself for this, it is preparing for this, and it is convinced that its existential enemy is Nato,” he said.
Macron made clear the national military service volunteers would not be sent to the front line.
“We must, in any case, immediately dispel any confusion that we are going to send our young people to Ukraine,” Macron said on Tuesday. “That’s not at all what this is about.”
France is not the only European nation bolstering its military capabilities.
Germany is redoubling its efforts to attract more recruits, notably via a new voluntary military service. The plan remains to be approved by parliament.
Belgium’s defence minister sent a letter this month to 17-year-olds to encourage them to sign up for military service next year, with the aim of selecting 500 candidates between 18 and 25 to launch the programme in September.
Poland has recently started rolling out a new voluntary military training programme and aims to train 100,000 volunteers per year from 2027 as it seeks to build an army of reservists with worries about Russia growing. It isn’t considering a return to universal military service, but rather a reserve system.
Ten EU countries have compulsory military service: Austria, Cyprus, Croatia, Denmark, Estonia, Finland, Greece, Latvia, Lithuania, and Sweden. Norway, which is not a member, has mandatory military service for both men and women. The length of service ranges from as little as two months in Croatia to up to 19 months in Norway.

data.title: Hospital video footage helps clear Chinese long jump world champion Wang Jianan of doping
data.description:
China’s world champion long-jumper Wang Jianan was cleared of doping after hospital video camera footage showed he had inadvertently inhaled a banned substance.
Wang became the first Asian man to win the world championships long jump gold when he leapt 8.36m at the world event in Eugene, Oregon, in 2022.
The China Anti-Doping Agency (Chinada) had said this week that Wang, 29, had not been negligent or at fault after failing an out-of-competition test last November and would not be banned.
Wang had inhaled particles of Terbutaline, used to prevent breathing problems, while accompanying a relative to hospital for nebuliser treatment.
Hospital security camera footage and patient records helped investigators track Wang’s movements in the hours before his drug test, the Athletics Integrity Unit (AIU) said in a statement this week.

AIU investigators said they had conducted a “comprehensive review of the documents and the CCTV video files” shared by China’s anti-doping agency and “concluded that there was nothing suspicious about them”.
